items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: What does freezing do to bacteria in food?
items.correct: Makes them dormant (slows or halts growth), but does not kill them.
items.misconception: Kills all bacteria in the food.
items.why: Students think freezing sterilises food. It just stops bacteria multiplying - they revive when thawed. That's why thawed food should be cooked promptly and not refrozen raw.

items.q: Should you wash raw chicken under the tap before cooking it?
items.correct: No - washing splashes Campylobacter bacteria around the sink and worktops; thorough cooking destroys the bacteria.
items.misconception: Yes - rinsing removes the bacteria from the surface of the chicken.
items.random_wrong: Yes - washing makes the chicken cook faster.
items.why: The Food Standards Agency specifically warns against washing raw poultry. Water droplets spread Campylobacter (the UK's most common cause of bacterial food poisoning) up to 50 cm around the sink. Cooking to 75°C core is what makes chicken safe.

items.q: If food looks and smells normal, is it guaranteed safe to eat?
items.correct: No - food poisoning bacteria such as Salmonella usually cause no change in taste, smell or appearance.
items.misconception: Yes - contaminated food always smells off or looks slimy.
items.random_wrong: Food is only unsafe if it changes colour.
items.why: Students confuse spoilage organisms (which cause off smells and sliminess) with pathogens. Dangerous bacteria can be present at illness-causing levels with zero sensory warning - which is why 'use by' dates and temperature control matter, not the sniff test.

items.q: How many portions of fruit and veg a day are recommended?
items.why: Correct answer: 5. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Eatwell Guide is from which country?
items.why: Correct answer: UK. “USA” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Recommended portions of oily fish per week?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 portion. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
